definition: To unfairly change the rules, criteria, or expectations after someone has already started trying to meet them.
usage_notes: Always negative — implies unfairness or bad faith
Common in business negotiations, politics, and project management
Implies the original targets were achievable but were changed to prevent success

etymology.origin_summary: From football (soccer) — literally moving the goalposts would make it impossible to score by the agreed rules.

examples: Every time we meet the requirements, they move the goalposts.
The client moved the goalposts halfway through the project, adding features we never agreed to.

confusions.difference: 'Raise the bar' can be positive (higher standards); 'move the goalposts' is always negative (unfair shifting).

literal_gloss: Physically relocating the goal in a sport so the other team can't score.
figurative_meaning: Changing the rules or criteria after someone has already begun working toward them.
misuse_notes: Don't use when standards are legitimately raised for good reason — this idiom implies unfairness.
